5 min read
Just now

--

Two of the design industry’s most significant 2026 trend reports arrived at the same conclusion independently, with direct implications for how founders should think about brand investment.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Brett Jordan on UnsplashWhat’s actually happening in the design industry right now
A significant and well-documented shift is underway in how visual work gets produced. Design trend coverage in 2026 shows non-designers, i.e, founders, marketers, freelancers, operators, are now building landing pages, prototypes, decks, and fully branded assets that previously required a product designer, a brand designer, and often a front-end developer working together.
The mechanism enabling this is straightforward: AI tools combined with shared prompt libraries and machine-readable brand guidance (design files that specify a brand’s colors, spacing rules, and visual patterns) allow non-designers to produce polished first drafts with minimal design-team involvement.
This represents a genuine democratization of visual production. The barrier that separated a business that could afford professional design from one that couldn’t is measurably lower than it was just two years ago.
The counter-movement this has triggered
What makes this moment particularly interesting is that it has produced a clear, independently documented counter-reaction across multiple major design research efforts.
Fast Company’s coverage of iF Design’s 2026 Trend Report frames it directly: “the most important design story of the year is not AI itself, but how design and designers are responding to it.”
As generative tools make creation faster, cheaper, and more uniform, the report finds that the most influential designers are moving consciously in the opposite direction, toward distinction, participation, imperfection, and genuine human connection. The report’s framing is explicit that this is not a rejection of AI, but a recalibration around shared humanity as the differentiator that remains, once efficiency is commoditized.
Adobe’s Creative Trends Forecast for 2026 reaches a strikingly similar conclusion through an entirely separate research process. The forecast identifies that the heavy influence of AI, AR technology, and gaming in daily life is, somewhat ironically, driving a backlash away from high-tech, and hyper-polished design, with creative output indexing more heavily toward organic, analog, and human-centered choices as a direct response.
Two significant, independently conducted research efforts; one from a design institution, and the other from the industry’s dominant creative software company, arrived at the same structural conclusion without coordinating. That convergence is a stronger signal than either report alone.
Why this is happening: the economics of scarcity
The underlying mechanism is a familiar one in any market where a previously scarce capability becomes widely accessible. When producing a polished, professional-looking visual asset required specialized skill, tools, and time, the existence of that polish itself functioned as a credibility signal; it demonstrated that a business had invested real resources into how it presented itself.
As AI tools make that polish achievable by nearly anyone in a fraction of the time, the polish itself stops functioning as a differentiator. It becomes an expected baseline rather than a signal of quality. This is precisely the dynamic behind Kittl’s 2026 trend research, which found that designers are actively working to escape what the report describes as “polished but predictable” output, which is a direct acknowledgment that visual competence alone no longer distinguishes meaningful work from generated sameness.
What actually differentiates now, according to the research
Across all of the major 2026 trend reports, the specific qualities identified as differentiating are consistent. They are distinction, imperfection, human-centered decision-making, tactile and sensory detail, and design that reflects a specific, genuine point of view rather than an optimized, generic one. Kittl’s research specifically documents a measurable rise in searches for hand-drawn and imperfect design elements as designers actively work to signal human authorship in a landscape saturated with AI-generated output.
The constant across these findings is that AI has not eliminated the value of strategic, human design thinking, it has only relocated where that value sits. Previously, value was distributed across both the strategic thinking behind a brand and the technical execution of that thinking. As execution becomes increasingly automatable, the value concentrates almost entirely in the strategic layer: positioning, honest self-knowledge about what a brand needs to communicate, and the specific point of view that AI cannot generate because it has no independent access to what a particular founder, business, or brand is actually trying to say.
What this means for brand investment decisions
For founders evaluating how to build or invest in their brand identity, this shift has a clear practical implication. The tools that execute visual production have become dramatically more accessible and should be used because refusing AI-assisted execution on principle produces no strategic advantage and simply costs time and money unnecessarily.
What cannot be shortcut through AI tools is the strategic foundation that gives that execution a real meaning: clear positioning that defines who a brand is for and what makes it different, a genuinely considered visual direction that reflects real decisions about audience and emotional register, and the specific, sometimes imperfect details that signal an actual human being made intentional choices about this brand rather than optimizing toward the most statistically likely output.
A brand built by generating polished assets without that underlying strategic work will look competent. It will also look, upon any real scrutiny, like a great many other AI-assisted brands built the same way, because when the same tools produce output for everyone, the absence of a distinct underlying point of view becomes the thing that’s actually visible.
The practical takeaway
The floor for visual competence has risen dramatically and will keep rising. That is a genuine benefit for businesses that previously couldn’t access quality design, but it also means visual competence alone is no longer a meaningful competitive advantage for any business, at any stage.
What remains scarce, and what the design industry’s own leading research consistently identifies as the actual differentiator going forward, is the strategic clarity and genuine point of view that has to be built before any tool, AI or otherwise, gets involved. That work has not gotten any easier. If anything, as the visible execution around it gets commoditized, it has become the only thing left worth competing on.

The Coast builds the strategic foundation; the positioning, the point of view, the thing that makes a brand unmistakably yours, before any tool, AI or otherwise, gets involved. If you want a brand built on more than polish, let’s talk.
